title: ICSE Class X Physics Practice Paper 10
subtitle: Comprehensive Review: Moments/Energy, Calorimetry/Phase Change, Lenses/Defects, Radioactivity, Power/Circuits
generalInstructions: Answers to this Paper must be written on the paper provided separately.
You will not be allowed to write during the first 15 minutes.
The time given at the head of this Paper is the time allowed for writing the answers.
Section A is compulsory. Attempt any four questions from Section B.
The intended marks for questions or parts of questions are given in brackets [ ].

questions.instruction: Choose the correct answers to the questions from the given options.

items.q: The product of the specific latent heat and the mass of the substance is a measure of the:
items.why_important: Tests the formula $\mathbf{Q = m L}$ and the meaning of latent heat.

items.q: The magnifying power of a simple microscope is maximum when the final image is formed at:
options: The least distance of distinct vision (D)
items.ans: The least distance of distinct vision (D)
items.why_important: Tests the condition for maximum magnification of a convex lens (Light).

items.q: If the length of a resistor wire is doubled, keeping its area of cross-section constant, its new resistance is:
items.why_important: Tests the relationship between resistance and length ($\mathbf{R \\propto L}$), a key concept in Electricity.
items.frequency: Highly frequent concept (2018, 2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

items.q: The energy possessed by a body due to its position or state is called:
items.why_important: Tests the definition of a fundamental energy type (W.E.P.).

items.q: The net force acting on a body moving in a horizontal circle is directed:
items.why_important: Tests the nature and direction of centripetal force (Mechanics).

items.q: The emission of a $\\gamma$-ray from a nucleus results in:
items.why_important: Tests the effect of $\\gamma$-decay on atomic and mass numbers (Modern Physics).

items.q: Which of the following is true for an ideal machine?
items.why_important: Tests the definition and condition for an ideal machine (Mechanics).

items.q: If the angle of incidence is $0^{\\circ}$ on a glass slab, the angle of refraction is:
items.why_important: Tests the condition for normal incidence (Light).

items.q: The objective property of sound that determines its loudness is:
items.why_important: Tests the subjective vs. objective property relationship (Sound).
items.frequency: Highly frequent core theory (2018, School Prelims)

items.q: An electric iron rated $1500 \\text{ W}, 220 \\text{ V}$ has a resistance of:
The energy change when a hydroelectric power plant operates is:
The essential component of a transformer that is laminated is the:
items.why_important: Tests the component responsible for reducing eddy current loss (Magnetism/Electricity).

items.q: The final image formed by a compound microscope is:
items.why_important: Tests the properties of the image formed by a compound microscope (Light).

items.q: Which of the following is true for a Beta ($\\beta$) particle?
options: It is the same as a Helium nucleus.
It travels slower than an Alpha particle.
items.why_important: Tests the identity of a $\\beta$-particle (Modern Physics).

items.q: The distance of $10 \\text{ cm}$ from the fulcrum is the load arm. The effort arm is $40 \\text{ cm}$. The VR is:

parts.text: The far point of a person is $400 \\text{ cm}$. Calculate the power of the lens required to restore normal vision. [3]
parts.why_important: Compulsory numerical on eye defects (Light).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent numerical (2023 Sample, School Prelims)

parts.text: State the Principle of Moments. Give one example of a Class I lever where the MA is greater than 1. [3]
parts.ans: Principle: For a body in rotational equilibrium, the sum of all clockwise moments about the pivot is equal to the sum of all anti-clockwise moments. Example: Crowbar, Claw Hammer.
parts.details: Checks the full statement of the principle and a real-life example of a force multiplier lever (Mechanics).
parts.why_important: Core definition and application of Moments.

parts.text: Why are copper wires generally used as connecting wires in electrical circuits? [2]
parts.ans: Copper has a very low resistivity (it is an excellent conductor), which minimizes the resistance of the connecting wires, ensuring minimal energy loss as heat.
parts.details: Checks the reasoning for material choice based on resistivity (Electricity).
parts.why_important: Standard reasoning question in Electricity.

parts.text: A block of wood floats on water. What is the work done by the buoyant force on the block when it is at rest? Justify your answer. [3]
parts.ans: Work done is zero. Justification: Work is done only if the point of application of the force undergoes a displacement in the direction of the force. Since the block is at rest (no displacement), the buoyant force does zero work on the block.
parts.why_important: Conceptual check on the definition of Work.

parts.text: State two factors that affect the pitch of a vibrating stretched string. Which is more important for a microphone: high sensitivity or wide frequency response? [3]
parts.ans: Factors: 1. Tension ($T$). 2. Length ($L$). 3. Linear density ($m$). Microphone: Wide Frequency Response.
parts.why_important: Core theory on Sonometer and advanced Sound concepts.

parts.text: Define the term calorie. State the difference between heat and temperature. [2]
parts.ans: Calorie: The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 g of water by 1 degree C. Difference: Heat is the total internal energy (form of energy). Temperature is the measure of degree of hotness (average KE of molecules).
parts.why_important: Fundamental definition pair (Heat).

parts.text: Draw a neat, labelled ray diagram to show how a convex lens is used as a magnifying glass. State the position of the object relative to the lens. [4]
parts.ans: Object placed between the Optical Centre (O) and Principal Focus (F1). Image is magnified, virtual, erect on the same side.
parts.why_important: Compulsory Ray Diagram (2024 School Prelims, 2025 Specimen Pattern).

parts.text: A motor runs on a $220 \\text{ V}$ supply and draws $5 \\text{ A}$. Calculate the cost of running it for $20 \\text{ hours}$ if the cost per unit is Rs. 5.50. [3]
parts.details: Power $P = VI = 1100 \\text{ W} = 1.1 \\text{ kW}$. Energy $= 1.1 \\times 20 = 22 \\text{ kWh}$. Cost $= 22 \\times 5.50 = \\text{Rs. } 121.00$.
parts.why_important: Standard numerical on commercial unit of energy (Electricity).

parts.text: Differentiate between alpha-rays and gamma-rays in terms of their deflection in an electric field. [2]
parts.ans: Alpha-rays: Positively charged, deflected towards the negative plate. Gamma-rays: Uncharged, not deflected in an electric field.
parts.why_important: Core comparison question (Modern Physics).

sectionB.instruction: Attempt any four questions from this Section.

parts.text: Calculate the amount of heat energy required to convert $50 \\text{ g}$ of ice at $-10^{\\circ}\\text{C}$ to water at $50^{\\circ}\\text{C}$.
sub: Heat required to raise temperature of ice to $0^{\\circ}\\text{C}$. [2]
Heat required to melt the ice at $0^{\\circ}\\text{C}$. [2]
Heat required to raise temperature of water to $50^{\\circ}\\text{C}$. [2]
parts.ans: Total Heat: $28350 \\text{ J}$
parts.why_important: Challenging multi-step numerical on phase change and specific heat (Calorimetry).
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section B (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: Give one reason why a burn from steam at $100^{\\circ}\\text{C}$ is more severe than a burn from boiling water at $100^{\\circ}\\text{C}$. [3]
parts.ans: Steam contains extra heat energy called Specific Latent Heat of Vaporisation (2268 J/g), which it releases when it condenses. This sudden release of additional heat causes a more severe burn.
parts.why_important: Standard reasoning question in Calorimetry.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent reasoning question (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: A wire of resistance $R$ is cut into four equal pieces. These pieces are then connected in parallel. Calculate the equivalent resistance of the combination in terms of $R$. [4]
parts.details: Each piece has resistance $R/4$. In parallel: $1/R_p = 4/(R/4) = 16/R$. So $R_p = R/16$.
parts.why_important: Challenging application of resistance and circuit laws (Electricity).

parts.text: What is the function of the main switch and the electricity meter in a household circuit? [4]
parts.ans: Main Switch: Allows the entire electrical supply to be cut off for safety. Electricity Meter: Measures the total electrical energy (in kWh) consumed.
parts.why_important: Core theory on House Wiring (Electricity).
parts.frequency: Frequent theory question (2025 Specimen, School Prelims)

parts.text: The power is transmitted at high voltage. Give a reason. [2]
parts.ans: To reduce energy loss during transmission. Since $P_{\\text{loss}} = I^2 R$ and $I$ is low when $V$ is high, high voltage transmission reduces heat energy lost.
parts.why_important: Standard reasoning question on Transformers/Transmission.

parts.text: A uniform plank of length $3 \\text{ m}$ is supported at its two ends, A and B. The reaction force at A is $50 \\text{ N}$, and the reaction force at B is $70 \\text{ N}$.
sub: Calculate the total weight of the plank and the load on it. [2]
Determine the position of the centre of gravity of the plank and the load from end A. [3]
parts.details: Total Weight $= R_A + R_B = 120 \\text{ N}$. Taking moments about A: $120 \\times x = 70 \\times 3$. $x = 1.75 \\text{ m}$.
parts.why_important: High-weightage application on Moments (determining C.G. position).
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section B (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: A stone of mass $2 \\text{ kg}$ is thrown vertically upwards with an initial velocity of $15 \\text{ m/s}$. Calculate the maximum height reached by the stone. (Use $g = 10 \\text{ m/s}^2$) [3]
parts.why_important: Numerical application of Conservation of Energy (W.E.P.).

parts.text: Differentiate between Centripetal and Centrifugal force (two points). [2]
parts.ans: Centripetal: Real force, directed towards the centre. Centrifugal: Fictitious/Apparent force, directed away from the centre.
parts.why_important: Core theory on forces in circular motion.

parts.text: State three properties of $\\alpha$-particles. Why do $\\alpha$-particles have high ionizing power? [5]
parts.ans: Properties: 1. Positively charged (+2e), high mass (4u). 2. Low penetrating power (stopped by paper). 3. Deflected by Electric/Magnetic fields. High Ionizing Power: Due to their large mass and high charge (+2e), they interact strongly with orbital electrons.
parts.why_important: Core theory on properties of alpha-particles.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent theory question (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is $2 \\text{ hours}$. If the initial count rate is $480$ counts/min, what will the count rate be after $6 \\text{ hours}$? [3]
parts.ans: $60 \\text{ counts/min}$
parts.why_important: Numerical application of the half-life concept.

parts.text: State the Law of Radioactive Disintegration. [2]
parts.ans: The rate of decay of a radioactive substance at any instant is directly proportional to the number of radioactive atoms present at that instant.
parts.why_important: Core definition (Modern Physics).

parts.text: An object $5 \\text{ cm}$ high is placed $30 \\text{ cm}$ from a convex lens of focal length $10 \\text{ cm}$.
sub: Calculate the position of the image. [3]
Calculate the height of the image. [3]
parts.ans: Position: $+15 \\text{ cm}$. Height: $-2.5 \\text{ cm}$.
parts.why_important: High-weightage numerical on the convex lens (Light).
parts.frequency: Compulsory numerical in Section B (2018, 2025 Specimen)

parts.text: Name the two main causes of Myopia. Draw a ray diagram to show how this defect is corrected. [4]
parts.ans: Causes: 1. Elongation of the eyeball. 2. Excessive curvature of the cornea/lens. Correction: Concave lens placed in front of the eye.
parts.why_important: Core theory/diagram on Myopia/Hypermetropia.
parts.frequency: Highly frequent theory/diagram question (2023 Sample, School Prelims)

parts.text: Draw a neat, labelled diagram of a single movable pulley system. State its MA and VR (ideal). [4]
parts.ans: Diagram: Single pulley with axle attached to Load. Rope runs under the pulley, one end fixed. MA = 2. VR = 2.
parts.why_important: Compulsory diagram for the Pulley system (Mechanics).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent diagram question (2017, School Prelims)

parts.text: A block and tackle system has a VR of 5. It is used to lift a load of $1200 \\text{ N}$ with an effort of $300 \\text{ N}$. Calculate the percentage efficiency of the machine and the work wasted when the effort moves $10 \\text{ m}$. [6]
parts.ans: Efficiency: $80\\%$. Work Wasted: $600 \\text{ J}$.
parts.details: MA = 1200/300 = 4. Efficiency = (4/5) x 100 = 80%. Work Input = 3000 J. Work Output = 2400 J. Wasted = 600 J.
parts.why_important: Challenging numerical on pulley system mechanics (Mechanics).
parts.frequency: Highly frequent numerical in Section B (2018, School Prelims)

parts.text: Distinguish between pitch and quality of sound (two points each). [4]
parts.ans: Pitch: 1. Subjective property. 2. Determined by Frequency. Quality: 1. Subjective property. 2. Determined by the waveform (overtones/harmonics).
parts.why_important: Core theory on Sound properties (essential distinction).

parts.text: A person standing between two parallel cliffs claps his hands. He hears the first echo after $1.5 \\text{ s}$ and the second echo after $3.5 \\text{ s}$. Calculate the distance between the two cliffs. (Speed of sound $= 340 \\text{ m/s}$) [4]
parts.details: Distance to closer cliff: $d_1 = (v \\times t_1)/2 = (340 \\times 1.5)/2 = 255 \\text{ m}$. Distance to farther cliff: $d_2 = (340 \\times 3.5)/2 = 595 \\text{ m}$. Total distance = $255 + 595 = 850 \\text{ m}$.
parts.why_important: Challenging numerical on Echo (Sound).

parts.text: State two conditions necessary for an echo to be heard. [2]
parts.ans: 1. The minimum distance between the source and the reflecting surface must be $17.2 \\text{ m}$ (for a time interval of at least $0.1 \\text{ s}$). 2. The reflecting surface must be large and hard.
parts.why_important: Core conditions for Echo (Sound).
